FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data with 1985 adolescents from Year 2-Year 4

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey; paper explicitly examines social media as exposure variable

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective models testing associations between social media use and sleep duration, moderated by brain activation (fMRI activity in nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula, putamen, caudate, middle frontal gyrus)

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract describes associations qualitatively (e.g., 'Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4') but does not report numeric coefficients, ORs, p-values, or effect sizes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis examining prospective associations across two years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of sleep research, 2026); no duplicate evidence

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/neural predictors (brain activation in reward and executive control regions) moderating associations between sleep and social media use. Per criterion (b), FT-EC7 only applies when predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual; neural features disqualify FT-EC7
notes: E3 applied: latent construct (brain activation pathways) with phone/SM-specific pathways extractable from abstract; moderation by neural features is reported separately. Missing numerics due to no PDF.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study data, N=11,878 children ages 9-10

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen time use' as examined variable but does not specify smartphone, social media, or any qualifying phone/SM exposure. Generic 'screen time' without device/app clarification fails FT-IC2

FT_IC3.evidence: No phone/SM variable specified; cannot assess association. FT-IC3 depends on FT-IC2 MET

FT_IC4.evidence: No phone/SM numeric coefficient reported; dependent on FT-IC2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional ABCD analysis with reported statistical tests and ORs

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology, 2026); no duplicate evidence

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because paper failed FT-IC2 (no smartphone/SM exposure). Paper is excluded at inclusion stage
notes: E18 applied: 'screen time use' is generic and unspecified device; does not qualify as smartphone or social media variable. Paper is excluded via FT-IC2 NOT_MET

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data across three consecutive waves (Years 2-4), approximately 7,260 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ) is outcome variable, but platform is unspecified (console, PC, mobile, or mixed). Per E17 and coder prompt, video games on unspecified platform = NOT_MET. Paper does not clarify whether gaming includes smartphone gaming or console/PC only

FT_IC3.evidence: No qualifying phone/SM exposure variable; cannot assess association. FT-IC3 depends on FT-IC2 MET

FT_IC4.evidence: No phone/SM numeric coefficient reported; dependent on FT-IC2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis using linear mixed-effects models with reported coefficients and mediation analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Frontiers in psychiatry, 2026); no duplicate evidence

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because paper failed FT-IC2 (unspecified video game platform, not smartphone/SM). Paper is excluded at inclusion stage
notes: E17 applied: video games with unspecified platform constitute NOT_MET for FT-IC2. Paper measures gaming addiction (VGAQ) as outcome, with ADHD and psychosocial mediators as predictors, but gaming platform is ambiguous. PDF available but platform ambiguity is not resolvable from abstract
